name: Anton Webern
abstract: Anton Webern (German: [ˈantoːn ˈveːbɐn] ; 3 December 1883 – 15 September 1945) was an Austrian composer, conductor, and musicologist whose modernist music was among the most radical of its milieu in its lyrical, poetic concision and use of then novel atonal and twelve-tone techniques. His approach was typically rigorous, inspired by his …

about_quotes.speaker: Roger Kellaway
about_quotes.evidence: I’ve listened to much too much twentieth century classical music... there’s too much Webern and Alban Berg and Prokofiev and Stravinsky... that have shaped my thinking about what sound is about.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roger Kellaway interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 14, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Paquito D'Rivera
about_quotes.evidence: My father... played... Webern. Those were two composers that he like very much... So he used to play... the Webern concerto for clarinet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Paquito D'Rivera — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Loren Schoenberg
about_quotes.evidence: It sounded like, like you know if Anton Webern, you know, was sick, and incapacitated. It was horrible. Atonal, without the other note.
about_quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019
